walkthrough.title: Give Each Player a Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Karuba set up. First, give each player one jungle board. These are the personal boards with a grid of empty spaces where you will be placing jungle tiles throughout the game. Each player places their board flat in front of them.

walkthrough.title: Sort Your Jungle Tiles
walkthrough.text: Each player has an identical set of 36 jungle tiles, numbered one through 36. Let's sort them face up so every player can quickly find any tile by number. You can arrange them in numerical order in rows or stacks, whatever works for you. Every player has the exact same tiles, which is key to how this game works.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Expedition Leader
walkthrough.text: Let's pick one player to be the expedition leader. This person will randomly draw tile numbers each round and announce them to the group. Everyone else will find the matching tile from their own set. The expedition leader still plays normally and makes all the same decisions as everyone else.

walkthrough.title: Place the Temples
walkthrough.text: Now the expedition leader places four colored temples on specific edge spaces along one side of their board. There are four colors, and each temple goes on a different space along the beach edge. Once the leader is happy with the placement, all other players must copy the exact same temple positions on their own boards. Everyone's boards must be identical.

walkthrough.title: Place the Adventurers
walkthrough.text: Next, the expedition leader places four colored adventurer meeples on edge spaces along the opposite side of the board from the temples. Each adventurer color matches one of the temple colors. Again, all other players copy the exact same positions. Each adventurer needs to reach the temple of the same color to score points.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Treasures
walkthrough.text: Let's place the treasures on the boards. Certain spaces on the board are marked with gold nugget or crystal symbols. Place gold nuggets and crystals on those marked spaces. Gold nuggets are worth two victory points each, and crystals are worth one. Finally, stack the temple treasure tokens at each temple in descending order: five on top, then four, three, and two. The first player to reach each temple across all players claims the highest remaining token.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Explore
walkthrough.text: Let's do a final check before we begin. Each player should have an empty board with four adventurers on one edge, four temples on the opposite edge, treasures on marked spaces, and 36 sorted tiles ready to go. The expedition leader should have their tiles in a bag or face down for random drawing. We are ready to start exploring the jungle.

Treasures on marked spaces, 36 tiles sorted and ready
Expedition leader's tiles ready for random draw

walkthrough.title: How a Round Works
walkthrough.text: Karuba is played over exactly 36 rounds, one for each tile. Every round, the expedition leader randomly draws one of their remaining tiles and announces the number to the group. All players then find the tile with the matching number from their own set. Everyone acts simultaneously, so there is no turn order.

walkthrough.title: The Core Choice: Place or Discard
walkthrough.text: Once everyone has found their tile, each player independently makes the same decision. You can either place the tile on your board or discard it to move an adventurer. This is the heart of Karuba. Every tile you place builds your path network, and every tile you discard moves an adventurer closer to their temple. You cannot do both with the same tile.

walkthrough.title: Placing a Tile
walkthrough.text: If you choose to place, put the tile on any empty space on your board. You may rotate the tile to any orientation you like. Paths on the tile do not need to connect to existing paths, but unconnected paths are useless for movement. Plan your placements carefully to build continuous routes from your adventurers to their matching temples.

walkthrough.title: Discarding a Tile to Move
walkthrough.text: If you choose to discard, remove the tile from the game and count the number of path exits on it. A straight tile has two exits and gives two steps of movement. A T-junction has three exits and gives three steps. A crossroads has four exits and gives four steps. Choose one adventurer and move them that many spaces along connected paths on your board.

walkthrough.title: Movement Rules
walkthrough.text: When moving an adventurer, they can only travel along connected, continuous path segments. An adventurer cannot pass through a space occupied by another adventurer because adventurers block each other. You must use all of your movement steps if the path allows it. If the path runs out before you have used all steps, the adventurer simply stops at the dead end.

walkthrough.title: Collecting Treasures
walkthrough.text: If an adventurer moves onto or through a space that has a gold nugget or crystal on it, they collect that treasure. Pick up the token and keep it near your board. Each treasure can only be collected once, so remove it from the board after collection. Gold nuggets are worth two victory points and crystals are worth one.

walkthrough.title: Reaching a Temple
walkthrough.text: When an adventurer reaches their matching colored temple, they stop immediately and claim the top temple treasure token from that temple's stack. The first player across all players to reach a particular color temple gets the five-point token. The second player gets four, the third gets three, and the fourth gets two. Racing to temples first is a huge part of the strategy.

walkthrough.title: Simultaneous Play
walkthrough.text: Remember that all players act at the same time. You and your opponents may make completely different choices with the same tile. One player might place tile number 15 while another discards it for movement. This means you cannot copy other players' strategies. You are building your own path network independently.

walkthrough.title: When the Game Ends
walkthrough.text: The game ends after all 36 tiles have been drawn and resolved. It can also end early if every player agrees that no further useful moves are possible. At that point, any adventurers who did not reach their matching temple score zero points for that temple.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Let's add up the scores. Count the value of any temple treasure tokens you claimed. Add two points for each gold nugget and one point for each crystal your adventurers collected. The player with the most total victory points wins. If there is a tie, the player who reached more temples wins. If still tied, you share the victory.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Jungle
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Karuba! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first several rounds together. We will follow along with four players who each make different decisions so you can see how the game plays out. Even if you have fewer players, follow along with all four to learn the key concepts.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what everyone has. Each player has an identical board with four adventurers on one edge and four matching temples on the opposite edge. Between them is an empty grid waiting for jungle tiles. The expedition leader has their tiles ready for random drawing. All players have their 36 tiles sorted and within reach.

walkthrough.title: Round One: First Tile Drawn
walkthrough.text: The expedition leader draws the first tile and announces the number. Everyone finds that tile from their set. Let's say it is a straight path tile with two exits. This is your very first decision in the game. Do you place it or discard it?

walkthrough.title: All Four Players Place
walkthrough.text: In the early game, placing tiles is almost always the right call. All four players decide to place this tile on their boards. Player one puts it near their blue adventurer, starting a path toward the blue temple. Player two rotates it and places it near their red adventurer. Player three and Player four each pick different spots. Remember, everyone starts building their own unique network.

walkthrough.title: Rounds Two and Three: Building Paths
walkthrough.text: The next two tiles are drawn. All four players continue placing tiles, extending their path networks. Player one is focusing on connecting blue and green adventurers toward their temples. Player two is building a wide central path that could serve multiple adventurers. These early rounds are all about laying the groundwork for your routes.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Discarding Too Early
walkthrough.text: On round four, Player three gets excited and discards a tile to move their yellow adventurer. The tile is a straight path, so yellow moves two spaces. But there is a problem. Player three's paths are barely started, and the adventurer hits a dead end after just two steps. That tile could have been placed to extend the path further. Now it is gone forever.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Before You Move
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here is important. Do not discard tiles for movement until your paths are well established. Every tile you discard is one fewer tile you can place to extend your network. In the early game, placement almost always beats movement. Build your paths first, then start racing your adventurers once the routes are clear.

walkthrough.title: A Crossroads Appears
walkthrough.text: A crossroads tile is drawn, the kind with four exits. This is a powerful tile. If placed, it creates a major intersection connecting paths in all four directions. If discarded, it gives four steps of movement, the maximum possible. Player one places it at a strategic junction where multiple paths will converge. Smart move since a crossroads is incredibly valuable as a connector.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Mistake: Ignoring the Temple Race
walkthrough.text: By round eight, Player one has a complete path to the blue temple and discards a T-junction tile for three steps of movement, racing the blue adventurer forward. Meanwhile, Player four has been placing tiles everywhere without any clear plan. Player four has no complete path to any temple yet, and the best temple tokens are about to be claimed.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Race for the Temples
walkthrough.text: The difference between a five-point and a two-point temple token is enormous. That is three victory points lost just for being slow. Always keep the temple race in mind when deciding whether to place or discard. If an opponent is close to completing a path, you need to prioritize getting your own adventurer to that same color temple before the best tokens are gone.

walkthrough.title: First Temple Reached
walkthrough.text: Player one discards another tile and moves the blue adventurer the rest of the way to the blue temple. Player one claims the five-point blue temple token. The other players will only be able to get four, three, or two points for reaching the blue temple now. This is a big moment in the game and shows why early path completion matters.

walkthrough.title: Collecting Treasures Along the Way
walkthrough.text: Player two has been building a path that runs through two gold nugget spaces and a crystal. When Player two finally starts moving their green adventurer along this route, they collect all three treasures. That is five extra victory points from treasures alone. Routing your paths through treasure spaces is always worth the slight detour.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Blocking Their Own Adventurer
walkthrough.text: Player three moves the red adventurer along a path, but the yellow adventurer is sitting in the middle of the same path from that early premature move. Red cannot pass through yellow. Player three is stuck and will have to move yellow out of the way first, wasting a future discard. This is the cost of moving adventurers without a plan.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Avoid Blocking Yourself
walkthrough.text: When you move adventurers, think about where they will end up. If two adventurers share any part of a path, one can block the other. Build separate routes when possible, or make sure you move adventurers in the right order so they do not jam up. Prevention is much easier than untangling a traffic jam mid-game.

walkthrough.title: The Mid-Game Shift
walkthrough.text: We are now about halfway through the 36 tiles. By this point, your path network should be mostly established. The balance shifts from placing tiles to discarding them for movement. Look at your board. Which paths are complete? Which adventurers have the best chance of reaching their temples? Focus your remaining discards on the adventurers that can actually finish the race.

walkthrough.title: Wrapping Up the Tutorial
walkthrough.text: Let's see where everyone stands. Player one reached two temples early and collected a few crystals. Player two has been treasure hunting and is about to reach their first temple. Player three lost time from blocking and early discards but is recovering. Player four finally has clear paths and is racing to catch up. The game continues until all 36 tiles are drawn.

walkthrough.title: Before You Explore
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on when to place versus discard.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to tile types and scoring.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun exploring Karuba!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Karuba players make. First, discarding tiles too early before paths are built, leaving adventurers stranded at dead ends. Second, ignoring the temple race and missing out on the five-point tokens. Third, blocking your own adventurers by moving them onto shared paths without thinking ahead. Fourth, spreading too thin and trying to build all four paths at once instead of prioritizing one or two. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: When to Place vs. Discard
walkthrough.text: The place-or-discard decision is the heart of Karuba. In the early game, almost always place tiles. You need a connected network before movement is useful. In the mid-game, start discarding once you have one or two complete routes. In the late game, discard aggressively since any tiles you place now will barely get used. A good rule of thumb is to place for the first 12 to 15 tiles, then gradually shift to discarding.

walkthrough.title: Focus on One or Two Adventurers First
walkthrough.text: Trying to build four complete paths simultaneously is a trap. Instead, focus on completing routes for one or two adventurers first. This lets you start racing those adventurers to their temples while other players are still building. Getting two adventurers to temples with five-point and four-point tokens is often better than getting all four adventurers to temples late with only two-point tokens each.

walkthrough.title: Tile Rotation Matters
walkthrough.text: Never forget that you can rotate tiles freely when placing them. A tile that looks useless in its default orientation might be perfect when rotated 90 or 180 degrees. Always check all four orientations before deciding whether to place or discard a tile. New players frequently discard tiles that would have been excellent connectors if they had simply rotated them.

walkthrough.title: Route Through Treasures
walkthrough.text: Gold nuggets are worth two victory points and crystals are worth one. A small detour to route a path through a gold nugget space is almost always worth it since two extra points can easily be the difference between winning and losing. Plan your path layouts from the start to pass through as many treasure spaces as practical without creating overly long routes that slow down your temple race.

walkthrough.title: Save High-Exit Tiles for Movement
walkthrough.text: Crossroads tiles with four exits give four steps of movement when discarded, which is the maximum. T-junction tiles give three steps. If you already have solid path connections in an area, consider saving these high-exit tiles for discarding later instead of placing them. Four steps of movement can cover a huge distance and get an adventurer to a temple in one move.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Temple Race
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to how close other players' adventurers are to their temples. If someone is about to claim a five-point token for a particular color, you need to decide whether to race for that same temple or accept the smaller token and focus elsewhere. Sometimes it is better to concede one temple race and focus on winning a different color where you have a head start.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Abandon an Adventurer
walkthrough.text: Sometimes an adventurer simply will not reach their temple. Maybe the path is blocked, too long, or there are not enough tiles left to build the route. When that happens, accept it and redirect your efforts toward adventurers who can still score. An unreached temple scores zero points, which is the same whether you almost got there or never tried. Focus your remaining discards on adventurers with realistic paths to their temples.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't discard tiles too early — paths must be built first
Don't ignore the temple race — 5 VP vs 2 VP is a 3-point swing
Don't block your own adventurers on shared paths
Don't spread thin — focus on 1-2 paths first

Late game: discard aggressively — placed tiles barely get used

summary.title: Know When to Abandon an Adventurer

Redirect discards to adventurers with realistic paths
Cutting losses early frees resources for winnable races

walkthrough.title: The Central Highway Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest approaches in Karuba is building a central highway that multiple adventurers share. Instead of creating four separate paths, build one wide corridor through the middle of your board, then branch off to individual temples near the edges. This is more tile-efficient because shared path segments do double or triple duty. Just be careful about adventurer blocking on the shared sections.

walkthrough.title: Tile Counting and Awareness
walkthrough.text: Since every player has the same 36 tiles, experienced players pay attention to which tile types remain. There are a limited number of crossroads, T-junctions, straights, and curves in the set. If you need a specific curve tile to complete a path and most of the curves have already been drawn, you know more are coming. Conversely, if you are hoping for a crossroads and most have already appeared, you may need an alternative plan.

walkthrough.title: Optimizing Tile Exits for Movement
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about tile exits from the very first draw. When you know you will eventually discard tiles for movement, you want to save the highest-exit tiles. Track how many crossroads and T-junctions remain in your undrawn pile. If you can afford to place a straight tile now and save a crossroads for later, you will get four movement steps instead of two. This planning over 36 rounds adds up to significantly more efficient movement.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and the Temple Race
walkthrough.text: Tempo in Karuba means being the first to complete a path and start moving. Every round you place a tile while your opponent is also placing, you gain nothing relative to them. But the round you start discarding while they are still building, you gain tempo. The goal is to finish your critical paths one or two rounds before your opponents. This means accepting imperfect paths if they are completed faster, rather than building the perfect route that takes three extra rounds.

walkthrough.title: Treasure Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Top players calculate the point value of treasure detours versus temple arrival speed. A gold nugget detour that adds two extra steps to your path costs you one round of movement, but gains two points. That is worth it. A crystal that adds three extra steps costs more movement than it gains in points. The math changes in the late game when tiles are running out. Skipping a crystal to claim a five-point temple token instead of a two-point token is a net gain of two points over collecting the crystal.

walkthrough.title: Reading Opponents
walkthrough.text: Even though Karuba is simultaneous, watching your opponents' boards gives you information. If an opponent is close to reaching the green temple, you know the five-point green token is about to be claimed. You can then decide whether to race for green with three points or redirect to a different temple where you can still get five. This awareness often separates experienced players from beginners who play in isolation.

walkthrough.title: Placement Flexibility
walkthrough.text: Experienced players keep their options open by placing tiles in ways that can serve multiple future paths. A T-junction placed at a midpoint can branch in multiple directions depending on later tiles. Avoid committing to a single rigid route too early. Leave one or two key spaces open that could connect to different temples depending on which tiles get drawn. This flexibility becomes more valuable as randomness in tile drawing makes rigid plans risky.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Calculation
walkthrough.text: In the last eight to ten rounds, switch to pure calculation mode. Count the remaining tiles and estimate how many movement steps you can still generate from discards. Calculate which adventurers can realistically reach their temples with the steps remaining. Focus all your discards on the highest-value targets. Reaching one more temple is almost always worth more than collecting scattered treasures. A five-point temple token plus a few treasures beats four crystals every time.

summary.title: The Central Highway Strategy
bullets: Build shared central corridor, branch to temples at edges
Shared path segments serve multiple adventurers = tile efficient
Watch for blocking on shared sections

Place low-exit tiles (straights = 2) when possible
Over 36 rounds, exit optimization adds up significantly

entries.definition: Each player's personal board with a grid of empty spaces for placing tiles. Adventurers start on one edge and temples sit on the opposite edge.
One of 36 numbered tiles showing path segments. Each player has an identical set. Tiles can be placed on the board or discarded for movement.
A colored meeple that moves along connected paths on the board. Each adventurer must reach the temple of the same color to score temple treasure tokens.
A colored piece placed on the beach edge of the board. When an adventurer reaches its matching temple, the player claims the top temple treasure token from that temple's stack.
Tokens stacked at each temple in descending order: 5, 4, 3, 2. The first player across all players to reach a temple claims the highest remaining value.
A treasure token placed on specific board spaces worth 2 victory points. Collected when an adventurer moves onto or through the space.
A treasure token placed on specific board spaces worth 1 victory point. Collected when an adventurer moves onto or through the space.
The designated player who randomly draws tile numbers each round and announces them to the group. The expedition leader plays normally and makes all the same decisions.
A tile with 2 path exits connecting opposite edges. When discarded, it provides 2 steps of movement. The simplest tile type.
A tile with 2 path exits connecting adjacent edges at a 90-degree angle. When discarded, it provides 2 steps of movement.
A tile with 3 path exits. When discarded, it provides 3 steps of movement. Useful for creating branching paths that can serve multiple adventurers.
A tile with 4 path exits connecting all four edges. When discarded, it provides 4 steps of movement — the maximum. Excellent as a central connector or a late-game movement boost.
One of the two choices each round. Put the drawn tile on any empty board space in any orientation to extend your path network.
One of the two choices each round. Remove the tile from the game and move one adventurer a number of steps equal to the tile's path exits (2, 3, or 4).
Paths must physically connect from tile to tile for an adventurer to travel along them. Dead-end paths waste tiles and block progress.
An adventurer cannot move through a space occupied by another adventurer. Players must plan routes or movement order to avoid their own adventurers blocking each other.
All players act at the same time each round. Different players may make different choices with the same tile number. There is no turn order.
When placing a tile, you may rotate it to any of four orientations. Always check all rotations before deciding to discard a tile.
The competition to reach each colored temple first. The first player to arrive gets 5 VP, second gets 4, third gets 3, fourth gets 2. A 3 VP swing between first and last.
The final score combining temple treasure tokens, gold nuggets (2 VP each), and crystals (1 VP each). Highest total wins. Tiebreaker: most temples reached.
